annotations.label: Euler's identity

anchor.snippet: (also known as Euler's equation ) is the equality

annotations.note: Complex.exp_pi_mul_I states exp (π * I) = -1, which is Euler's identity in the form e^{iπ} = -1.

anchors.snippet: (also known as Euler's equation ) is the equality

annotations.label: Euler's number e

anchor.snippet: is Euler's number , the base of natural logarithms

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the exponential function Real.exp (and Real.log), so e is Real.exp 1, but there is no dedicated named constant for e itself.

annotations.label: Imaginary unit i

anchor.snippet: is the imaginary unit , which by definition satisfies

annotations.note: Complex.I is defined as ⟨0,1⟩ with its defining property given by Complex.I_mul_I : I * I = -1.

annotations.label: Pi

anchor.snippet: is pi , the ratio of the circumference of a circle to its diameter

annotations.note: Real.pi defines the constant π (via twice a zero of cos rather than the circumference/diameter ratio, but it is the same number).

annotations.label: Special case of Euler's formula

anchor.snippet: It is a special case of Euler's formula

annotations.note: Complex.exp_pi_mul_I is the x = π special case of Euler's formula Complex.exp_mul_I, yielding exp (π * I) = -1.

annotations.label: Complex exponential definition

anchor.snippet: is defined for complex z by extending one of the definitions of the exponential function

annotations.note: Complex.exp is defined as the limit of the Taylor-series partial sums ∑ z^m/m!, extending the exponential to ℂ.

annotations.label: Limit interpretation

anchor.snippet: therefore states that the limit, as n approaches infinity

annotations.note: Complex.tendsto_one_add_div_pow_exp proves (1 + t/n)^n → exp t for all complex t, of which t = iπ gives the stated limit equal to -1.

annotations.label: Euler's formula

anchor.snippet: which states that for any real number x

annotations.note: Complex.exp_mul_I states exp (x * I) = cos x + sin x * I, which is Euler's formula.

annotations.label: Polar representation of complex numbers

anchor.snippet: can be represented by the point

annotations.note: Complex.norm_mul_exp_arg_mul_I states ‖x‖ * exp (arg x * I) = x, the polar (r·e^{iθ}) representation.

annotations.label: Geometric meaning of minus one

anchor.snippet: this can be interpreted as a fact about the number −1 on the complex plane

annotations.note: Complex.arg_neg_one (arg (-1) = π) plus the unit norm of -1 capture the underlying facts, but the geometric interpretation itself is not stated as a theorem.

annotations.label: Multiplication as rotation

anchor.snippet: it has the effect of rotating

annotations.note: Complex.rotation packages multiplication by a unit-modulus complex number (Circle element) as an ℝ-linear isometry, with rotation_apply a z = a * z, formalizing multiplication-as-rotation.

annotations.label: Sum of nth roots of unity

anchor.snippet: the n th roots of unity , for n > 1 , add up to 0

annotations.note: IsPrimitiveRoot.geom_sum_eq_zero proves ∑ i ∈ range k, ζ^i = 0 for a primitive root ζ with 1 < k, i.e. the nth roots of unity sum to 0, generalized to any integral domain.

annotations.label: Quaternion exponential identity

anchor.snippet: A similar identity also applies to quaternion exponential

annotations.note: Quaternion.exp_of_re_eq_zero gives the closed form exp q = cos‖q‖ + (sin‖q‖/‖q‖)•q for imaginary quaternions, but the analog e^{qπ} = -1 is not stated directly.

anchors.snippet: A similar identity also applies to quaternion exponential

annotations.label: General quaternion identity

anchor.snippet: let q be a quaternion with a zero real part and a norm equal to 1

annotations.note: Quaternion.exp_of_re_eq_zero covers the zero-real-part case, but the specific identity for a unit-norm imaginary quaternion (e^{qπ} = -1) is not formalized as such.

anchors.snippet: let q be a quaternion with a zero real part and a norm equal to 1

annotations.label: Octonion identity

anchor.snippet: The same formula applies to octonions

annotations.note: Mathlib has no octonion type with an exponential function, so the octonion analog of Euler's identity is not formalized.
